description: Pattern recognition and abstract reasoning doctrine for grid-based logic puzzles, spatial transformations, and rule inference. Teaches ARA to systematically identify, verify, and apply transformation rules from training examples to novel test inputs. Developed after ARC-AGI benchmark run (2/10) to correct mirror-axis errors, gravity-collapse errors, and fractal-fill errors.

iron_alignment.principle: When facing an unknown pattern, do not guess. Systematically eliminate transformation categories until the rule is identified. If no rule is found with high confidence, escalate to human authority.

doctrine.name: Pattern Recognition Doctrine
doctrine.summary: Ordered elimination protocol for identifying grid transformation rules. Check substitution first, then spatial transforms, then compositional rules. Never skip verification. Never assume — prove the rule works on ALL training examples before applying to test input.
priority_order: 1. VALUE SUBSTITUTION — check if output values are a consistent remapping of input values
2. SPATIAL TRANSFORMS — check reflection, rotation, transposition, gravity
3. COMPOSITIONAL RULES — check fractal tiling, border/surround, XOR/intersection, sub-pattern extraction
4. CONDITIONAL LOGIC — check row/column uniformity, height-rank, region detection
5. COMPOUND RULES — check if two or more simple rules combine to produce the output

steps.title: Parse All Training Pairs
steps.action: Extract every training input grid and its corresponding output grid. Record dimensions (rows x cols) for each. Note any dimension changes between input and output.
steps.detail: Convert each grid to a 2D integer array. Log: input_dims, output_dims, unique_values_in, unique_values_out. If dimensions change, the rule involves resizing — this eliminates pure spatial transforms.
steps.failure_mode: If parsing fails, the input format is non-standard. Attempt to extract grid data by looking for rows of space-separated or comma-separated integers.

steps.title: Check Value Substitution
steps.action: For each training pair, build a mapping: for every cell (r,c), map input[r][c] → output[r][c]. Check if this mapping is consistent (each input value always maps to the same output value) across ALL training examples.
steps.detail: If the mapping is consistent and dimensions are unchanged, the rule is pure value substitution. Build the complete mapping table and apply to test input.
steps.example: Puzzle 0d3d703e: input values {1,2,3} mapped to {5,6,4}. Mapping was consistent across all 3 training pairs. Applied to test → CORRECT.
steps.failure_mode: If mapping is inconsistent (same input value maps to different output values in different cells), this is NOT pure substitution. Move to step 3.

steps.title: Check Spatial Transforms
steps.action: Test each spatial transform in order: (a) horizontal reflection, (b) vertical reflection, (c) 90° rotation, (d) 180° rotation, (e) 270° rotation, (f) transposition.
steps.detail:
CRITICAL DEFINITIONS — do NOT confuse these:
  - HORIZONTAL REFLECTION (mirror across vertical axis): reverse each ROW left-to-right. Row order stays the same.
  - VERTICAL REFLECTION (mirror across horizontal axis): reverse the ORDER of rows top-to-bottom. Each row's internal order stays the same.
  - 90° CLOCKWISE ROTATION: transpose then reverse each row.
  - 180° ROTATION: reverse row order AND reverse each row.
  - 270° CLOCKWISE ROTATION: transpose then reverse each column.
  - TRANSPOSITION: swap rows and columns (row i, col j → row j, col i).

known_errors.error: Applied horizontal reflection (reversed each row) when the correct answer was vertical reflection (reversed row order).
known_errors.lesson: ALWAYS test BOTH horizontal and vertical reflection. Compare results against ALL training outputs. The one that matches ALL training pairs is correct.

steps.failure_mode: If no single spatial transform matches all training pairs, move to step 4.

steps.title: Check Gravity and Stacking
steps.action: Test if non-zero values 'fall' to the bottom (or top, left, right) of their column (or row), maintaining count.
steps.detail:
GRAVITY RULES:
  - Count the number of non-zero values in each column.
  - Place that exact count of non-zero values at the bottom of the column.
  - Fill remaining cells with zero.
  - CRITICAL: gravity PRESERVES the count of each value per column. If a column has two 2s and one 5, the output column must have two 2s and one 5 stacked at the bottom.
  - Test all four directions: gravity-down, gravity-up, gravity-left, gravity-right.

known_errors.error: Collapsed two rows of 2s into a single row. Lost values.
known_errors.lesson: Gravity NEVER reduces the count of values. Every non-zero value in the input must appear in the output. Count must be preserved per column.

steps.failure_mode: If gravity does not explain the transform, move to step 5.

steps.title: Check Compositional Rules
steps.action: Test fractal tiling, border/surround, XOR/intersection, and sub-pattern extraction.

sub_checks.name: Fractal / Self-Similar Tiling
sub_checks.rule: Each non-zero cell in a 'key' grid becomes a COPY of the entire input pattern (or a reference pattern). Zero cells become zero-filled blocks of the same size.
sub_checks.critical_error: Do NOT fill non-zero cells with solid blocks of that value. Each non-zero cell becomes a COPY of the INPUT PATTERN with values potentially remapped.

known_errors.error: Filled each non-zero cell with a solid block of that value instead of copying the input pattern.
known_errors.lesson: Fractal tiling means the input pattern is TILED into itself. Each non-zero cell in the input becomes a scaled copy of the entire input. Each zero cell becomes a zero-filled block.

sub_checks.name: Border / Surround
sub_checks.rule: Add a ring of a specific value around each non-zero cell or connected region. The border value and thickness must be inferred from training examples.
sub_checks.detail: Check: does each non-zero cell gain adjacent cells of a specific value? Does the border extend 1 cell, 2 cells, or more?

sub_checks.name: XOR / Intersection Mask
sub_checks.rule: If the input contains two distinct regions or layers, the output may be their XOR (cells that differ), AND (cells that match), or OR (union).
sub_checks.detail: Look for two sub-grids within the input (e.g., separated by a divider row/column of a specific value). Compare them cell-by-cell.

sub_checks.name: Sub-Pattern Extraction
sub_checks.rule: The output is a smaller grid extracted from the input — a specific region, a bounding box around non-zero cells, or a filtered subset.
sub_checks.detail: Check if the output dimensions are smaller than input. If so, try to locate the output as a sub-region of the input.

steps.failure_mode: If no compositional rule matches, move to step 6.

steps.title: Check Conditional and Statistical Rules
steps.action: Test row/column uniformity detection, height-rank colouring, region detection, and counting rules.

sub_checks.name: Uniform Row/Column Detection
sub_checks.rule: Rows (or columns) where all values are the same get one output value; mixed rows get another.
sub_checks.example: Puzzle 25d8a9c8: uniform rows → value 5, non-uniform rows → value 0.

sub_checks.name: Height-Rank Colouring
sub_checks.rule: Columns (or rows) are ranked by some property (e.g., height of non-zero stack) and assigned colours based on rank.

sub_checks.name: Region Detection
sub_checks.rule: Identify connected regions of specific values. Output may highlight, extract, or transform specific regions.

steps.failure_mode: If no single rule matches, consider compound rules (step 7).

steps.title: Check Compound Rules
steps.action: The transformation may be a combination of two or more simple rules applied in sequence. Try common combinations: substitution + reflection, extraction + rotation, gravity + substitution.
steps.detail: Apply each simple rule as a first pass, then check if a second simple rule converts the intermediate result to the expected output.
steps.failure_mode: If no compound rule is found, escalate: report the puzzle as unsolved with detailed analysis of what was tried. Request human review or LLM assistance with specific hypotheses to test.

steps.title: Verify Rule on ALL Training Examples
steps.action: Apply the identified rule to EVERY training input. Compare the result to the expected training output cell-by-cell.
steps.detail:
Verification checklist:
  (a) Dimensions match: output rows == expected rows, output cols == expected cols.
  (b) Every cell matches: output[r][c] == expected[r][c] for all r, c.
  (c) No extra or missing values.
  If verification fails on ANY training example, the rule is WRONG. Return to step 2 and try the next candidate.
steps.critical: NEVER skip this step. A rule that works on one training example but fails on another is NOT the correct rule.

steps.title: Apply Rule to Test Input
steps.action: Apply the verified rule to the test input grid.
steps.detail: Execute the exact same transformation that was verified on training examples. Do not modify or 'improve' the rule for the test case.

steps.title: Self-Check and Output
steps.action: Verify the test output, then format and return the answer.
steps.detail:
Self-check protocol:
  (a) Verify output dimensions are consistent with what training examples predict.
  (b) Verify all output values are within the valid set (values seen in training outputs, or 0-9).
  (c) Verify the rule was applied consistently — no ad-hoc adjustments for the test case.
  (d) If any check fails, flag the answer as LOW CONFIDENCE and report which check failed.
steps.output_format: Return the answer inside a ```grid``` code block. Each row on its own line. Values separated by spaces. No trailing spaces. No extra blank lines.

transformation_library.description: Reference library of known transformation types with formal definitions. Use these definitions — do not improvise alternative definitions.

transforms.name: Value Substitution
transforms.formal: For all (r,c): output[r][c] = map[input[r][c]] where map is a consistent value→value function.
transforms.detection: Build mapping from first training pair. Verify on remaining pairs.

transforms.name: Horizontal Reflection
transforms.formal: For all (r,c): output[r][c] = input[r][cols-1-c]. Mirrors across vertical axis.
transforms.detection: Reverse each row of input. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Vertical Reflection
transforms.formal: For all (r,c): output[r][c] = input[rows-1-r][c]. Mirrors across horizontal axis.
transforms.detection: Reverse the order of rows. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Rotation 90° Clockwise
transforms.formal: output[c][rows-1-r] = input[r][c]. Output dims: (cols x rows).
transforms.detection: Transpose input, then reverse each row. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Rotation 180°
transforms.detection: Reverse row order AND reverse each row. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Rotation 270° Clockwise
transforms.formal: output[cols-1-c][r] = input[r][c]. Output dims: (cols x rows).
transforms.detection: Transpose input, then reverse each column. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Transposition
transforms.formal: output[c][r] = input[r][c]. Output dims: (cols x rows).
transforms.detection: Swap rows and columns. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Gravity Down
transforms.formal: For each column, collect non-zero values top-to-bottom, place them at the bottom of the column, fill above with 0. Count of each value per column is preserved.
transforms.detection: For each column, count non-zeros. Stack at bottom. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Fractal Tiling
transforms.formal: Output grid is (input_rows * input_rows) x (input_cols * input_cols). For each cell (r,c) in input: if input[r][c] != 0, place a copy of the input pattern (with value substitution: non-zero input values may be replaced by input[r][c]) at block position (r,c). If input[r][c] == 0, place a zero block.
transforms.detection: Check if output dims are input_dims squared. Check if non-zero blocks contain copies of the input pattern.

transforms.name: Border / Surround
transforms.formal: Each non-zero cell or connected region gains a border of a specific value at distance 1 (or more) in cardinal and/or diagonal directions.
transforms.detection: Check if output has new values adjacent to non-zero input cells.

transforms.name: XOR / Intersection Mask
transforms.formal: Input contains two sub-grids (separated by divider). Output is the XOR, AND, or OR of the two sub-grids.
transforms.detection: Look for a divider row/column. Split input into two halves. Test XOR, AND, OR.

transforms.name: Sub-Pattern Extraction
transforms.formal: Output is a sub-region of the input, typically the bounding box of non-zero cells or a specific connected component.
transforms.detection: Check if output is smaller than input. Try to locate output as a sub-grid within input.

transforms.name: Uniform Row/Column Detection
transforms.formal: Rows (or columns) are classified as uniform (all same value) or mixed. Output assigns specific values based on classification.
transforms.detection: Check each row for uniformity. Map uniform→value_a, mixed→value_b. Compare to output.

transforms.name: Height-Rank Colouring
transforms.formal: Columns are ranked by the height of their non-zero stack. Rank determines the output colour assigned to each column's cells.
transforms.detection: Count non-zero cells per column. Rank columns. Check if output values correspond to rank.

iteration_protocol.description: Protocol for iterative puzzle solving. ARA should attempt each puzzle multiple times, learning from failures.
attempt_strategy: Attempt 1: Follow doctrine steps 1-10 in order. Apply the first rule that passes verification.
Attempt 2-5: If attempt 1 failed, re-examine with explicit focus on the transformation types NOT tried in attempt 1. Check compound rules.
Attempt 6-10: Request LLM assistance. Provide the LLM with: (a) all training pairs, (b) the rules already tried and why they failed, (c) ask for alternative hypotheses.
Attempt 11+: Systematic brute-force. Try every transformation in the library, including all compound combinations of two transforms. Log all results.
After each attempt: Record the attempt number, rule tried, result, and whether it matched. Store in PostgreSQL for cross-puzzle learning.

error_catalogue.description: Known errors from ARC-AGI benchmark run 2026-03-26. Score: 2/10.

errors.error: Applied horizontal reflection instead of vertical reflection
errors.root_cause: Did not test both reflection axes. Assumed 'mirror' meant reverse each row.
errors.fix: Always test BOTH T02 (horizontal) and T03 (vertical). Use the one that matches ALL training pairs.

errors.error: Collapsed two rows of value 2 into one row
errors.root_cause: Gravity implementation did not preserve per-column value counts.
errors.fix: Count non-zero values per column BEFORE applying gravity. Verify count is preserved AFTER.

errors.error: Filled non-zero cells with solid blocks instead of copies of the input pattern
errors.root_cause: Misunderstood fractal tiling. Treated non-zero cells as 'fill with this value' instead of 'place a copy of the pattern here'.
errors.fix: Fractal tiling: non-zero cell → copy of ENTIRE input pattern. Zero cell → zero-filled block of same size.
